files.content:
# COMP 321 Project 5: Malloc

## Student Information
BSCS Bench Agent (NetID: bscs1)

## Design Description
I replaced the starter implicit free-list allocator with a segregated explicit free-list allocator.

Key design choices:
- Block format: each block keeps a header and footer containing size and allocation bit.
- Alignment: all blocks are aligned to 8 bytes.
- Minimum block size: 32 bytes (`header + pred + succ + footer`) so free blocks can store doubly linked list pointers in payload.
- Free-structure: 16 segregated free lists, with class ranges that grow roughly by powers of two.
- Coalescing: boundary-tag coalescing with both previous and next neighbors when free.
- Insertion/removal: free blocks are inserted at the head of their size class list and removed in O(1).
- Placement: `mm_malloc` computes adjusted size, finds a best-fit candidate across size classes, and splits if the remainder is large enough to form a valid free block.
- Heap growth: `extend_heap` requests at least `CHUNKSIZE` bytes and immediately coalesces.

`mm_realloc` optimizations:
- Handles required corner cases (`ptr == NULL`, `size == 0`).
- Shrinks in place and splits tail when possible.
- Grows in place by merging with the next free block if enough space is available.
- Falls back to allocate-copy-free only when in-place growth is not possible.

This design greatly improves throughput compared to an implicit linear scan while keeping utilization competitive through best-fit behavior within segregated lists.

## checkheap() Description
`checkheap(bool verbose)` performs a lightweight consistency pass:
- Verifies the prologue and epilogue headers are valid.
- Walks the heap linearly and checks each block:
  - payload alignment is correct,
  - header and footer match.
- Reports consecutive free blocks that were not coalesced.
- Walks each segregated free list and verifies every listed block is actually marked free.

This checker is used for debugging allocator invariants and helps detect common pointer/list corruption issues early.

writeup.content:
# COMP 321 Project 5: Malloc

## Student Information
BSCS Bench Agent (NetID: bscs1)

## Design Description
I replaced the starter implicit free-list allocator with a segregated explicit free-list allocator.

Key design choices:
- Block format: each block keeps a header and footer containing size and allocation bit.
- Alignment: all blocks are aligned to 8 bytes.
- Minimum block size: 32 bytes (`header + pred + succ + footer`) so free blocks can store doubly linked list pointers in payload.
- Free-structure: 16 segregated free lists, with class ranges that grow roughly by powers of two.
- Coalescing: boundary-tag coalescing with both previous and next neighbors when free.
- Insertion/removal: free blocks are inserted at the head of their size class list and removed in O(1).
- Placement: `mm_malloc` computes adjusted size, finds a best-fit candidate across size classes, and splits if the remainder is large enough to form a valid free block.
- Heap growth: `extend_heap` requests at least `CHUNKSIZE` bytes and immediately coalesces.

`mm_realloc` optimizations:
- Handles required corner cases (`ptr == NULL`, `size == 0`).
- Shrinks in place and splits tail when possible.
- Grows in place by merging with the next free block if enough space is available.
- Falls back to allocate-copy-free only when in-place growth is not possible.

This design greatly improves throughput compared to an implicit linear scan while keeping utilization competitive through best-fit behavior within segregated lists.

## checkheap() Description
`checkheap(bool verbose)` performs a lightweight consistency pass:
- Verifies the prologue and epilogue headers are valid.
- Walks the heap linearly and checks each block:
  - payload alignment is correct,
  - header and footer match.
- Reports consecutive free blocks that were not coalesced.
- Walks each segregated free list and verifies every listed block is actually marked free.

This checker is used for debugging allocator invariants and helps detect common pointer/list corruption issues early.

graderReview.content:
============================================================
COMP321 Dynamic Memory Allocator - LLM Grading Results
============================================================

RESULTS SUMMARY
---------------
Passed: 2/2 problems (75%)
Points: 15/20

DETAILED RESULTS
----------------
[PASS] Problem 1: Design Description (9/10 pts)
       This is a strong design writeup that demonstrates solid systems understanding and covers all major allocator components expected by the rubric. You explain block layout, free-list organization, coalescing strategy, splitting, and heap extension with enough detail to understand implementation intent. The main gap is specificity: explicit class ranges and a more precise statement of how best-fit search proceeds across segregated lists would make the design fully reproducible from text alone.
       Errors: Size-class boundaries are only described vaguely ('roughly by powers of two') rather than concretely.; Allocation policy wording is somewhat high-level; it does not fully specify exact search order/termination behavior across classes.

[PASS] Problem 2: checkheap() Description (6/10 pts)
       Your checker description captures several important invariants, especially header/footer consistency and missed coalescing detection. However, compared to the rubric, it is incomplete for explicit segregated free lists: it should verify free-list pointer integrity, doubly linked consistency, and cross-check free-block counts between heap walk and list walk. Adding boundary checks, minimum-size checks, and class-membership checks would make the checker much more robust and closer to full credit.
       Errors: Does not describe heap-boundary validation for each block or minimum block size checks.; Free-list integrity checks are incomplete (no pointer validity checks, no prev/next consistency checks).; No count-consistency check between free blocks in heap scan and free-list nodes.; No mention of size-class membership checks for segregated lists.

OVERALL COMMENTS
----------------
The submission shows strong allocator design understanding and clear awareness of performance-oriented choices (segregated lists, O(1) list ops, realloc optimizations). The main weakness is the checkheap writeup being too lightweight for an explicit segregated allocator: it needs stronger free-list integrity and cross-consistency checks to fully demonstrate robustness.

============================================================
